sections.header: Verses 1–8 — “The LORD Has a Day of Vengeance”

heb.paragraph: "The LORD is angry with all nations; his wrath is on all their armies. He will totally destroy them" (v.2). The most ferocious judgment oracle in Isaiah. The starry host dissolves; the heavens roll up like a scroll (v.4) — Revelation 6:14 quotes this verbatim. God’s sword is bathed in blood in heaven (v.5). "The LORD has a day of vengeance (nāqām), a year of retribution, to uphold Zion’s cause" (v.8). Edom is the target: Bozrah’s streams turn to pitch, dust to sulphur — Sodom-imagery applied to Edom (vv.6, 9).

notes.note: "All the stars of the heavens will be dissolved and the sky rolled up like a scroll." Revelation 6:14 quotes this directly. The de-creation imagery: what God rolled out in creation, he rolls up in judgment.

"My sword has drunk its fill in the heavens. The LORD has a day of vengeance, a year of retribution, to uphold Zion’s cause." The vengeance is FOR Zion. Judgment on the nations is mercy toward the oppressed.
"A day of vengeance, a year of retribution." Day vs year: vengeance is brief; retribution is measured. Even in fury, God is proportional.

refs.note: The winepress of God's wrath — Revelation develops Isaiah's slaughter imagery.
Birds summoned to eat the flesh of kings — echoing Isaiah's cosmic feast of judgment.
Oracle against Mount Seir (Edom) — Ezekiel extends Isaiah's Edom-judgment.
Obadiah's entire book is an Edom-oracle, developing themes from Isaiah 34.

hist.context: Chapter 34 shifts to cosmic judgment, with Edom as representative enemy. The 'host of heaven' dissolving (v.4) uses apocalyptic language — stars falling like withered leaves. Edom's judgment is described as sacrifice (v.6): Yahweh's sword is sated with blood. The specific mention of 'Bozrah' (v.6), Edom's capital, grounds the cosmic imagery in geography. Edom's treachery during Jerusalem's fall (cf. Obadiah, Ps 137:7) made it paradigmatic enemy.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt notes the universal summons: 'nations,' 'peoples,' 'earth,' 'world.' This is not merely judgment on Edom but on the principle Edom represents — hostility to God's people and purposes.
The sacrifice imagery is brutal but theological: judgment is not random violence but holy offering. The LORD's sword is 'sated' — the language of ritual completeness. Divine justice is satisfied.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs reads chapter 34 as counterpart to chapter 35: judgment and salvation form a diptych. Edom's desolation (ch.34) is paired with Zion's restoration (ch.35). The two cannot be separated.

The command to 'search the scroll of the LORD' (v.16) is remarkable. Childs sees this as the earliest canonical consciousness: Isaiah's words are scripture to be searched for confirmation of prophecy's fulfillment.

sections.header: Verses 9–17 — “It Will Lie Desolate; No One Will Pass Through It Again”

heb.paragraph: "God will stretch out over Edom the measuring line of chaos (tōhū) and the plumb line of desolation" (v.11). The vocabulary of Genesis 1:2 (tohu wabohu) applied as a judgment tool. Edom is un-created: thorns overgrow palaces, jackals fill fortresses (v.13). Desert creatures, wild goats, night creatures inhabit it (vv.13–14). "Look in the scroll of the LORD and read: none of these will be missing" (v.16). The prophecy is self-referencing: read this scroll in the future and verify its fulfilment.

notes.note: "The measuring line of chaos and the plumb line of desolation." God uses construction tools to build destruction. The irony: measuring and plumbing, which build cities, now produce wasteland.
"Desert creatures, wild goats, night creatures. The owl will nest there." The anti-Eden: where God’s garden once grew, wilderness reclaims.

"Look in the scroll of the LORD and read: none of these will be missing." Isaiah appeals to his own written prophecy as future evidence. The first explicit reference to a prophetic scroll as a self-verifying document.

refs.note: Babylon's desolation with wild animals — the same imagery applied to another enemy.
Jeremiah's Edom oracle uses similar desolation imagery.
'Esau I have hated' — Malachi confirms Edom's judgment and Israel's election.
Fallen Babylon as haunt of demons and unclean birds — the desolation tradition continues.

hist.context: The desolation imagery (vv.9-15) depicts Edom becoming like Sodom: pitch and sulfur, smoke forever. The wild creatures listed (jackals, owls, ravens, etc.) emphasize complete reversal: where civilization stood, only wilderness remains. The 'line of chaos and plumb line of desolation' (v.11) parodies building: instead of constructing, God measures out destruction. This becomes paradigmatic for how prophets describe enemy judgment.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt notes the 'day of vengeance' (v.8) and 'year of retribution' language. This is not arbitrary wrath but judicial response to Edom's treatment of Zion. Covenant curses fall on covenant enemies.

The appeal to 'the scroll of the LORD' assumes Isaiah's prophecies are already authoritative text. Oswalt sees early canon-consciousness: the written word has normative status for discerning God's purposes.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs emphasizes that Edom represents 'the world' opposed to God's purposes. The specific enemy becomes type of all enemies. This hermeneutical move enables later readers to apply the text beyond original context.

Childs finds here the earliest clear statement of canonical authority within the prophetic corpus. The 'scroll of the LORD' is to be searched — it contains reliable predictions that will be fulfilled. Scripture interprets history.

ppl.name: LORD
ppl.text: The LORD has a day of vengeance, a year of retribution, to uphold Zion’s cause…

ppl.name: nations
ppl.text: Judgment on the nations is mercy toward the oppressed…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVMy sword has drunk its fill in the heavens; see, it descends in judgment on Edom, the people I have totally destroyed.ESVFor my sword has drunk its fill in the heavens; behold, it descends for judgment upon Edom, upon the people I have devoted to destruction.NIVThe Lord is angry with all nations; his wrath is on all their armies. He will totally destroy them, he will give them over to slaughter.ESVFor the LORD is enraged against all the nations, and furious against all their host; he has devoted them to destruction, has given them over for slaughter.

rec.text: ""All the stars of the heavens will be dissolved and the sky rolled up like a scroll." Revelation 6:14 quotes this directly. The de-creation imagery: what God rolled out in creation, he rolls up in jud…"

rows.label: Measuring Line of Chaos — 34:9–17
rows.text: "The measuring line of CHAOS" (tohu). Anti-Eden. "Look in the SCROLL and read: none will be missing."

hebtext.note: "The LORD is angry with all nations; his wrath is on all their armies. He will totally destroy them" (v.2). The most ferocious judgment oracle in Isaiah. The starry host dissolves; the heavens roll up like a scroll (v.4) — Revelation 6:14 quotes this verbatim. God’s sword is bathed in blood in heaven (v.5). "The LORD has a day of vengeance (nāqām), a year of retribution, to uphold Zion’s cause" (v.8). Edom is the target: Bozrah’s streams turn to pitch, dust to sulphur — Sodom-imagery applied to Edom (vv.6, 9).

"God will stretch out over Edom the measuring line of chaos (tōhū) and the plumb line of desolation" (v.11). The vocabulary of Genesis 1:2 (tohu wabohu) applied as a judgment tool. Edom is un-created: thorns overgrow palaces, jackals fill fortresses (v.13). Desert creatures, wild goats, night creatures inhabit it (vv.13–14). "Look in the scroll of the LORD and read: none of these will be missing" (v.16). The prophecy is self-referencing: read this scroll in the future and verify its fulfilment.

themes.note: Judgment peaks at 10: heavens rolled up, creation reversed, Edom un-made. Rev 6:14 quotes directly. The measuring line of chaos (tohu).

coaching.tip: Chapter 34's universal judgment matches chapter 24's cosmic scope — 'all the nations' gather for slaughter, the heavens dissolve, the stars fall (vv. 2–4). The parallels are deliberate. Isaiah's Apocalypse (24–27) and this judgment oracle form bookends around the woe oracles (28–33). The architecture says: everything between these cosmic panels operates under the shadow of final reckoning.

'Search through the scroll of the LORD and read' (v. 16). Isaiah appeals to his own written prophecy as verifiable — come back and check whether this happened. He assumes literacy, written records, and accountability to the text. Isaiah isn't just delivering oracles; he's creating Scripture and knows it. The self-referential moment reveals a prophet conscious of his canonical role.
